payload.markdown:
## Learning Goals

By the end of this page, students should be able to:

- recognize their professor
- list some of the topics covered by social psychology

### Social psychology is everywhere. 

Your friendships, your group chats, even your late night scrolling. Who we are and how we treat each other can't be boiled down to a single TikTok trend, or even a single variable in an experiment. 

### People are complicated. 

There are always multiple reasons for what we do. In this course, we will use an intersectional lens to examine the richness of our social lives from every angle. Race, gender, age, social class, sexual orientation, and many others. 

### Because real life isn't one-dimensional. 

We aren't ever just one thing. Let me give you an example. 

### Picture yourself at age seven or eight. 

Now imagine you were the only biracial kid in your school photo, with a huge afro, and a strong belief that soon, racism would be a thing of the past. 

### That was me. The epitome of naivety. 

One weekend, a friend invited me to his home. His family ran a motel. They lived just behind the main office. My mom, who's white, dropped me off. 

### Everything seemed perfectly normal. 

While playing in the lobby, a Pakistani man pulled in and asked for a room. He was told no rooms were available, even as he pointed out the vacancy sign. My friend's mom said it was a mistake, that they had no more rooms. 

### And I believed her. 

After all, why would a business owner turn away a customer and lose money? But later, when a white couple arrived, they were given a room immediately. When my dad showed up to pick me up, I heard:

### "That's your dad?" 

After that, I was never invited over again. 

### When you hear my story, what explanations pop into your mind? 

Would you believe the "no rooms available" line? What would you say to your friend the next day at school? This is where social psychology has a lot to say. It explores so many of the processes I witnessed on that day at my friend's motel. 

- Prejudice, 
- stereotyping, 
- discrimination, 
- identity, 
- moral reasoning, 
- family conflict, 
- relationships, 
- persuasion, 
- cognitive dissonance. 

I found myself asking, "How could my friend and his family treat people of color so differently?" The search for that answer inspired my undergraduate thesis and led me to work with social psychologists dedicated to uncovering and reducing implicit biases. And my hope is that this course will challenge you to think critically, to question assumptions, and maybe even launch your own research project.

## Learning Goals

By the end of this page, students should be able to:

- Define Social Psychology
- Define Psychology

### Let's start by thinking about how social psychology is related to psychology in general, as well as some of the other subfields of psychology. 

And once we understand that, I want to briefly think about how psychology fits into the family of sciences. 

### Let's start with a definition of psychology. 

> Psychology is the scientific study of behavior and the mind. 

So psychologists are interested in everything we can see a person do, their behavior, and also everything we think is going on in a person's mind, even if we can't see it. 

### Thinking just about behavior, psychologists are interested in

- smiling, 
- learning to walk, 
- how we do on tests, 
- how we treat one another, including actions like 
    - helping others and 
    - discriminating against others. 
    
### And psychologists are also interested in how we treat ourselves. 

Thinking just about the mind, psychologists are interested in:

- how we make decisions, 
- how we learn, 
- how we love, 
- how we forgive, and 
- generally how we feel. 

Obviously, that's a lot of things to study, and it's only a very partial list. 

### No one psychologist studies everything that could be considered psychology. 

Instead, there are subfields that describe a psychologist's focus or interest. 

> For example, developmental psychology is the scientific study of the mind and behavior across the lifespan from when we were born until we die. 

> Cognitive psychology is the scientific study of basic mental abilities such as perception, learning, and memory. 

### So what about social psychology? 

There is no ultimate definition of social psychology, or any psychology, but I like this one. 

> The scientific study of the way in which people's thoughts, feelings, and behaviors are influenced by the real or imagined presence of other people. 

In other words, how do other people influence our behavior and our mind, and how do we influence them? I want to highlight what I think is an often overlooked part of this definition, though. 

### That's the imagined presence of others. 

So it's not just people, but thinking about people that influences our behavior and thoughts. 

> Consider this. Does your boss need to stare over your shoulder for you to start working harder? Or is just thinking about your boss staring over your shoulder enough to get you working harder? 

The answer is probably sometimes yes and sometimes no. Social psychologists want to know when the answer is yes and when it's no. 

The final thing I want to do in this video is highlight one similarity among these definitions, and that is the phrase "scientific study." 

### What is a scientific study?

For now, I want to suggest that just as there are many subfields of psychology, psychology is one of many subfields of science. Before you start the next video, I want to challenge you to think about what the common thread might be that ties all these different disciplines together.

## Add these to your notes

1. The definition of psychology
2. The definition of social psychology
3. The definition of developmental psychology
4. The definition of cognitive psychology

## Learning Goals

By the end of this page, students should be able to:

- Distinguish science from pseudoscience
- Define Science
- Define the scientific method

Social psychology and all subfields of psychology are part of the family of sciences like chemistry, physics, and biology. 

- Chemists want to learn about how chemicals interact with each other. 
- Physicists want to learn about the fundamental nature of reality. 
- Biologists want to learn about the diversity of life. 
- Psychologists want to learn about the mind and behavior. 

### But so do astrologers. 

Astrology is concerned with the exact same subject matter as psychology. Our personalities, our behaviors, and our relationships. 

### But I wouldn't call astrology a science. 

To understand why, let's look at the definition of science. 

> Science is the systematic gathering of data to provide descriptions of events taking place under specific conditions, enabling researchers to explain, predict, and control events. 

Both astrologers and psychologists purport to help people explain, predict, and control events. 

### So that isn't the difference between them. 

The difference lies in the first half of the definition, the systematic gathering of data. 

> In other words, what makes something a science is that it follows the scientific method. 

So what makes psychology a science and astrology a pseudoscience is that psychologists use the scientific method while astrologers do not. 

I like to use a very broad definition of the scientific method to acknowledge that different scientific disciplines use different methodologies and that those methodologies change over time. 

> The scientific method is the steps scientists take to gather and verify information, answer questions, explain relationships, and communicate findings. 

### So there is no one way to do science, but all science involves gathering and verifying information. 

It also involves the use of theories to explain relationships. And to do science, you must share what you learn with others. A pseudoscience may take some of these steps, but not all of them. For example, astrology has a theory that explains relationships--why people have certain personalities. And it shares this theory with the entire world every day via newspapers and the internet. Some astrologers may even gather data to test their theory. But this is one of the reasons astrology isn't a science. There is no requirement to gather information. 

### And most importantly, when information contradicts the theory, there is no requirement to update the theory. 

So those are two key points that make psychology a science. 

### Psychologists gather and verify data and then update their theories when new data contradicts them. 

Psychological theories are being updated all the time. 

### Before you go to the next page, I challenge you to look up your horoscope and think about it as a testable prediction. 

How could you gather information that would falsify the prediction? 

## Add these to your notes

1. The definition of science
2. The definition of pseudoscience
3. The definition of scientific method
4. A table of differences between science and psuedoscience

## Learning Goals

By the end of this page, students should be able to:

- Define theory
- Define hypothesis
- Recognize and apply Fiske's seven signs of a good theory

### Imagine researchers in Lab A gather and publish some data.

Then researchers in *Lab B* do the same, but it's slightly different.
Some of the variables are the same, but some of them are different.
Then *Lab C* gathers and publishes even more data.
They have one variable in common with Lab A and B, but maybe they found a different pattern.

### What are we to make of all this data?

Some of it seems consistent, and some of it seems inconsistent.
How do we make sense of it all?

### That's what theories are for.

> Theories take all the existing data and provide some general principles that explain how they all make sense together.

But that's only the first part of the definition of a theory.
If the first part of the definition describes the relationship between theories and data we already have,
as in, it helps us to explain it,
the second part describes the relationship between theories and data we hope to have.

### Theories help us make new predictions that we can test by gathering new data.

Those predictions are what we call hypotheses.
> So a theory is a set of general principles that attempts to explain and predict behavior or other phenomena.

### A hypothesis is a causal prediction.

> Hypotheses are generated from theories.

And in fact, that's one of the criteria we use to judge the value of a theory.

### Does it generate testable causal predictions?

Fiske suggests seven signs that a theory is good.
And I think it's useful to think about how the theory of astrology stacks up.

*  1. A good theory posits causal relationships or generates hypotheses.

Astrology knocks this one out of the park.

Every single day there are new hypotheses for how every single person's day should go.

* 2. A good theory attempts to be coherent.

Here's a place where astrology falls short.
There just isn't any coherent explanation for how the sun's apparent position relative to arbitrarily defined constellations at the time of your birth somehow affects your personality.

* 3. A good theory tells a good story.

Astrology has this in spades.
This might seem like an irrelevant attribute for a good theory to have,
but the truth is that if a theory doesn't tell a compelling story that makes sense to people, then researchers won't test it.
And even if the theory is correct, it won't lead to new knowledge.

### This sign probably contributes a great deal to astrology's popularity.

Even knowing astrology is inaccurate, I still find it fun to check my horoscope every now and then.

* 4.  A good theory is parsimonious, which means it's as simple as possible while explaining as much data as possible.

And what could be simpler than astrology as an explanation for behavior, personality, and relationships?

* 5. A good theory is testable.

Another way to say this is that a good theory can be falsified.

### Astrology gets a check here because it generates simple, testable predictions that just happen to be wrong.

* 6. A good theory proves fertile, which just means that it gets used.

Researchers test hypotheses generated from the theory, which leads to new data, and then new hypotheses, and then more data again, and so on.
Astrology doesn't do that. You test astrology once, it doesn't work, and then you aren't allowed to revise the theory because it's a pseudoscience and no more research gets conducted.

* 7. A good theory solves problems, and astrology, like psychology, attempts to solve the most basic and fundamental problems humans face,
understanding ourselves and understanding each other.

In the next video, I'll discuss how to generate hypotheses or causal predictions.

Before that, though, I encourage you to think about the psychological theories you've learned in other classes.
How would you test them? Are those theories falsifiable?

## Add these to your notes

1. The definition of theory
2. The definition of hypothesis
3. Fiske's 7 signs of a good theory

## Learning Goals

By the end of this page, students should be able to:

- Define correlational methods
- Define experimental methods
- Describe the relation between power and perspective-taking

### Social psychologists have conducted a number of really clever studies investigating power, 

Here's one hypothesis that illustrates the differences between correlational and experimental research designs: 

> If a person feels powerful, then their perspective-taking will be low. 

Maybe you've met someone like this, a boss, a manager, a parent, or even a teacher who wasn't good at taking the perspective of people they had power over. Galinsky and colleagues systematically investigated this hypothesis using both *correlational* and *experimental* methods. 

### Using the correlational method, 

Galinsky and colleagues observed or measured the associations between power and perspective-taking. They did this by having participants complete two scales: 

* one that measured participants' sense of power, called the sense-of-power scale, 
* and one that measured their perspective-taking, which was part of the interpersonal reactivity index. 

Results supported their hypotheses because the higher a person's sense of power, the lower they scored on perspective-taking. 

### It is important to keep in mind that correlational studies cannot, on their own, be used to determine causality. 

So even though Galinsky and colleagues predicted that power influences perspective-taking, this correlational study only tells them that power and perspective-taking were related to one another. 

### To make a causal claim, researchers should use the experimental method. 

In this method, researchers manipulate one variable and then observe whether the manipulation corresponds to changes in the other variable.  

## Add these to your notes

1. The definition of correlational methods
2. The definition of experimental methods
3. The relation between power and perspective-taking

questions.prompt: What is psychology?
answers: The scientific study of social groups
The scientific study of behavior and the mind
The scientific study of people
The scientific study of culture and language
questions.explanation: The source text defines psychology as the scientific study of behavior and the mind.

questions.prompt: Which definition best matches social psychology?
answers: The scientific study of the mind and behavior across the lifespan
The scientific study of basic mental abilities such as perception and memory
The scientific study of the way people's thoughts feelings and behaviors are influenced by the real or imagined presence of other people
The scientific study of biological systems in the brain
questions.explanation: The text defines social psychology in terms of influence from the real or imagined presence of other people.

questions.prompt: How did the video define developmental psychology?
answers: The scientific study of behavior in groups
The scientific study of the mind and behavior across the lifespan from birth until death
The scientific study of emotion regulation in adulthood
The scientific study of memory and perception
questions.explanation: The passage states that developmental psychology studies the mind and behavior across the lifespan.

questions.prompt: How did the video define cognitive psychology?
answers: The scientific study of basic mental abilities such as perception learning and memory
The scientific study of behavior across the lifespan
The scientific study of how people influence one another
The scientific study of how people think
questions.explanation: The passage gives perception learning and memory as examples of the basic mental abilities studied in cognitive psychology.

questions.prompt: Which definition of science is the closet match to Dr. Phills’ definition?
answers: The use of theory to explain personality
The systematic gathering of data to describe events under specific conditions and enable explanation prediction and control
Any attempt to explain and predict events
The study of chemicals, physics, biology, or psychology
questions.explanation: Dr. Phills defines science as the systematic gathering of data to provide descriptions of events under specific conditions, enabling researchers to explain, predict, and control events.

questions.prompt: According to Dr. Phills what primarily distinguishes science from pseudoscience?
answers: Whether it studies people and behavior
Whether it follows the scientific method and systematically gathers data
Whether it tries to predict events
Whether it uses theories
questions.explanation: The source says the key difference lies in the systematic gathering of data and following the scientific method.

questions.prompt: How does Dr. Phills define the scientific method?
answers: The one standard procedure used by all scientists
The steps scientists take to gather and verify information answer questions explain relationships and communicate findings
A set of laboratory techniques used by all scientists
A process for predicting events
questions.explanation: Dr. Phills gives a broad definition: the steps scientists take to gather and verify information, answer questions, explain relationships, and communicate findings.

questions.prompt: Why does Dr. Phills identify astrology as a pseudoscience rather than a science?
answers: It does not concern personality or relationships
It never shares ideas with the public
It does not require theory revision when data contradict it
It cannot explain or predict events
questions.explanation: Dr. Phills notes that astrology may explain relationships and share theories, but it does not require updating theory when contradicted by evidence.

questions.prompt: Why is the ability to explain predict and control events not enough by itself to make something a science?
answers: Because only chemistry and physics do those things
Because pseudosciences can also claim to explain predict and control events
Because science is not only about prediction
Because science is only about describing events
questions.explanation: Both astrologers and psychologists purport to explain, predict, and control events, so that cannot be the defining difference.

questions.prompt: According to Dr. Phills what is the main job of a theory?
answers: To replace all data with intuition
To take existing data and provide general principles that explain how the findings make sense together
To list every observation without interpretation
To summarize experiments
questions.explanation: Dr. Phills says theories take all the existing data and provide general principles that explain how the findings fit together.

questions.prompt: How does Dr. Phills define a theory?
answers: A set of general principles based on results from multiple studies
A set of general principles that attempts to explain and predict behavior or other phenomena
A summary of general principles derived from a collection of experiments
A collection of causal claims
questions.explanation: Dr. Phillsexplicitly defines a theory as a set of general principles that attempts to explain and predict behavior or other phenomena.

questions.prompt: What does Dr. Phills say hypotheses are?
answers: A set of general principles that explain and predict behavior
A list of every variable in a study
Causal predictions generated from theories
General principles that organize causal predictions
questions.explanation: Dr. Phills states that hypotheses are generated from theories and that a hypothesis is a causal prediction.

questions.prompt: What is the relationship between theories and hypotheses according to Dr. Phills?
answers: Hypotheses replace theories once enough data are collected
Theories are generated from hypotheses
Hypotheses are generated from theories
Theories and hypotheses are unrelated
questions.explanation: Dr. Phills directly says hypotheses are generated from theories.

questions.prompt: Which of the following is one of Fiske's signs of a good theory?
answers: Theories are cautious. They avoid prediction.
It posits causal relationships or generates hypotheses
It may be testable but doesn't have to be
It describes variables
questions.explanation: Fiske's first sign is that a good theory posits causal relationships or generates hypotheses.

questions.prompt: According to Fiske, what does it mean for a theory to be coherent?
answers: It has a compelling story
It has an explanation whose parts fit together logically
It generates many hypotheses
It is creative
questions.explanation: Fiske treats coherence as having an explanation that makes sense together, contrasting astrology as lacking a coherent explanation.

questions.prompt: What does Fiske mean when she says a good theory is parsimonious?
answers: It's as simple as possible
It uses simple language
It is as simple as possible while explaining as much data as possible
It generates simple hypotheses
questions.explanation: Dr. Phills defines parsimony as being as simple as possible while explaining as much data as possible.

questions.prompt: What does Fiske say about a testable theory?
answers: It is logically coherent
It can be falsified
It should sound scientifiuc
It should make causal claims
questions.explanation: Dr. Phills says another way to say a good theory is testable is that it can be falsified.

questions.prompt: Which list correctly captures Fiske's seven signs of a good theory?
answers: Causal,  tells a good story, parsimonious, testable, fertile, solves problems, permanent
Generates hypotheses, coherent, tells a good story, parsimonious, testable, fertile, solves problems
Simple, popular, descriptive, intuitive, broad, fixed, untestable
Generates hypotheses, coherent, tells a good story, parsimonious, testable, unquestionable, popular
questions.explanation: Fiske lists seven signs: posits causal relationships or generates hypotheses, coherent, tells a good story, parsimonious, testable, fertile, and solves problems.

questions.prompt: What do correlational methods do?
answers: Manipulate one variable to cause change in another
Observe or measure the associations between variables
Randomly assign participants to conditions
Eliminate all alternative explanations
questions.explanation: Correlational methods observe or measure the associations between power and perspective-taking.

questions.prompt: What pattern did Galinsky and colleagues’ correlational study on power find?
answers: Higher power was associated with higher perspective-taking
Lower power was associated with lower perspective-taking
Higher sense of power was associated with lower perspective-taking
There was no relation between power and perspective-taking
questions.explanation: The higher a person's sense of power, the lower they scored on perspective-taking.

questions.prompt: When should researchers use the experimental method?
answers: When they want to make a causal claim
When they want to describe a relationship
When they cannot measure variables
When they want to avoid manipulating variables
questions.explanation: Researchers should use the experimental method to make a causal claim.

questions.prompt: Which best describes the experimental method?
answers: Researchers observe two variables
Researchers manipulate one variable and measure another
Researchers compare two theories
Researchers ask participants to complete scales
questions.explanation: Dr. Phills defines the experimental method as manipulating one variable and then observing whether the manipulation corresponds to changes in the other variable.
